stem: A 58-year-old man with chronic alcohol use disorder is admitted to a Hunter New England LHD ward 14 hours after his last drink, following an elective hernia repair postponed for medical optimisation. He drinks approximately 1.5 bottles of wine daily. He is now diaphoretic, has fine tremor, complains of nausea, and is anxious. He has had two previous episodes of severe alcohol withdrawal, one with a generalised tonic-clonic seizure. He weighs 78 kg. He has no significant hepatic dysfunction (LFTs and INR normal).

data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, fine outstretched-hand tremor, anxious, alert, oriented; HR 108, BP 152/92, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air, temp 37.4.
data_table.Investigations: Bloods: U&E normal, glucose 5.8 mmol/L, magnesium 0.78 mmol/L, ALT 45, INR 1.0; no infection screen abnormalities.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial pharmacological regimen?

options.text: Diazepam-loading regimen guided by CIWA-Ar scoring, plus parenteral thiamine 300 mg IV daily for 3 days
options.rationale: Australian guidance (NSW Health, eTG Addiction Medicine) supports diazepam-loading guided by the revised Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol (CIWA-Ar) in patients with preserved hepatic function and a history of severe withdrawal or seizures. Diazepam's long half-life provides a 'self-tapering' profile and reduces seizure risk. Parenteral thiamine (300 mg IV daily for 3 days) precedes glucose administration to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy precipitated by carbohydrate-driven thiamine consumption.
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines 2008
Australian guidelines for the treatment of alcohol problems NHMRC 2021

options.text: Lorazepam 1 mg PO every 4 hours as needed
options.rationale: Lorazepam (short-acting, no active metabolite) is preferred in significant hepatic dysfunction; this patient has normal LFTs and diazepam-loading is preferred for previous seizure history. Would be correct in severe liver disease (Source: eTG).

options.text: IV haloperidol 5 mg for agitation
options.rationale: Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold and do not address the GABAergic deficit driving withdrawal. Would be wrong as primary agent (Source: eTG).

options.text: Naltrexone 50 mg daily started today
options.rationale: Naltrexone is for relapse prevention once withdrawal is complete and the patient is abstinent; it does not treat acute withdrawal. Would be wrong timing (Source: AMH).

options.text: IV glucose 10% infusion before thiamine to maintain blood sugar
options.rationale: Glucose before thiamine in a thiamine-deficient patient can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy; thiamine must precede glucose. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health).

explanation.summary: Alcohol withdrawal management in patients with normal hepatic function uses CIWA-Ar-guided diazepam loading (or scheduled diazepam in the severe-history group). The long half-life of diazepam provides a self-tapering effect, reducing both rebound and seizure risk. Severe hepatic dysfunction shifts choice to lorazepam (no active metabolite, glucuronidation rather than CYP). Parenteral thiamine 300 mg IV daily for 3 days precedes any carbohydrate load to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy. CIWA-Ar score >=15 is severe and warrants more intensive management.
key_points: Diazepam-loading via CIWA-Ar in preserved hepatic function
Lorazepam in severe hepatic dysfunction (glucuronidation)
Parenteral thiamine 300 mg IV daily x 3 days before glucose
Long-acting benzodiazepines reduce seizure risk in withdrawal
Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold - avoid as primary agent
explanation.pearls: The classical revised CIWA-Ar covers 10 items including tremor, agitation, anxiety, sweats, nausea, headache, tactile/auditory/visual disturbances, and orientation; scoring guides individualised benzodiazepine dosing.

sources.label: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines 2008
NHMRC Australian guidelines for the treatment of alcohol problems 2021

subtopic_detail: Diazepam-loading regimen for severe alcohol withdrawal history

stem: A 62-year-old woman with established alcoholic cirrhosis (Child-Pugh class B, MELD 14) is admitted to a Central Coast LHD ward for management of alcohol withdrawal 18 hours after her last drink. She is encephalopathic at baseline (West Haven grade 1, asterixis intermittent), with INR 1.8, bilirubin 60 micromol/L, albumin 28 g/L. CIWA-Ar is currently 14.

data_table.Examination: Mild jaundice, asterixis, abdomen soft with mild ascites, alert but slow.
data_table.Investigations: Bilirubin 60, albumin 28, INR 1.8, ALT 80, AST 120, platelets 110, creatinine 70.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate benzodiazepine choice for her alcohol withdrawal?

options.text: Lorazepam 1-2 mg PO/IV every 1-2 hours symptom-triggered via CIWA-Ar, with close encephalopathy monitoring
options.rationale: In significant hepatic dysfunction (cirrhosis, Child-Pugh B/C, elevated INR, encephalopathy), lorazepam is preferred because it is metabolised by conjugation (glucuronidation) rather than CYP-mediated oxidative phase I metabolism. This avoids the prolonged sedation and active-metabolite accumulation seen with diazepam (its active metabolites - desmethyldiazepam, oxazepam, temazepam - accumulate and prolong sedation, worsening encephalopathy assessment). Symptom-triggered dosing reduces total exposure.
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Guidelines 2008
eTG Addiction Medicine: Withdrawal in liver disease

options.text: Diazepam-loading 20 mg PO every 1-2 hours until sedated
options.rationale: Diazepam and its active metabolites accumulate in hepatic dysfunction, prolonging sedation and confounding encephalopathy assessment. Would be wrong in this hepatic context (Source: eTG).

options.text: Chlordiazepoxide 25 mg every 6 hours
options.rationale: Chlordiazepoxide has the same long-acting accumulation issue in liver disease as diazepam. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Midazolam infusion at 2 mg/hour
options.rationale: Midazolam IV infusion is for ICU-level sedation, not first-line ward withdrawal management; risks respiratory depression. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Avoid benzodiazepines entirely and use clonidine alone
options.rationale: Clonidine treats autonomic symptoms but does not prevent withdrawal seizures or delirium tremens; benzodiazepines remain the GABAergic substitute. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

explanation.summary: Benzodiazepine choice in alcohol withdrawal is shaped by hepatic function. Diazepam and chlordiazepoxide undergo phase I oxidative metabolism (CYP) and have active long-acting metabolites that accumulate in liver dysfunction. Lorazepam, oxazepam, and temazepam undergo only conjugation (glucuronidation), which is largely preserved in cirrhosis - hence the LOT mnemonic. Symptom-triggered dosing (CIWA-Ar) reduces total exposure compared with fixed-schedule dosing. Encephalopathy monitoring is critical to detect over-sedation.
key_points: Significant liver dysfunction: lorazepam preferred (glucuronidation only)
LOT mnemonic = Lorazepam, Oxazepam, Temazepam - safe in liver disease
Symptom-triggered CIWA-Ar dosing reduces total exposure
Diazepam/chlordiazepoxide accumulate in hepatic dysfunction
Encephalopathy monitoring critical for over-sedation detection
explanation.pearls: Hepatic encephalopathy and benzodiazepine over-sedation can look identical; structured monitoring (CIWA-Ar plus separate encephalopathy assessment) is essential.

sources.label: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Guidelines 2008

subtopic_detail: Lorazepam symptom-triggered dosing in cirrhosis withdrawal

stem: A 47-year-old man with chronic alcohol use disorder is admitted to a Hunter New England LHD ED with intoxication and confusion. He is malnourished and his BMI is 19. He has no acute ocular signs or ataxia. The team plans his admission for observation and management of likely impending withdrawal, with IV maintenance fluids that include 5% dextrose.

data_table.Examination: Smell of alcohol, ataxic gait, slurred speech, oriented to person only; no focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 4.2 mmol/L, electrolytes within normal limits.

lead_in: Which thiamine regimen and sequencing most closely follows Australian guidance to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy?

options.text: Parenteral thiamine 300 mg IV daily for 3 days BEFORE any glucose-containing fluid, then 100 mg oral daily
options.rationale: Australian guidance (eTG, NSW Health) and the Royal College of Physicians prophylactic protocol specify parenteral thiamine 300 mg IV daily for 3 days for patients at high risk of Wernicke (alcohol use disorder, malnutrition, hyperemesis, post-bariatric surgery) before any carbohydrate load. Glucose given to a thiamine-deficient brain consumes residual thiamine in pyruvate decarboxylation and transketolase reactions, precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy. Oral thiamine 100 mg daily continues for at least 3-6 months after the parenteral phase.
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Guidelines 2008
Royal College of Physicians Wernicke guideline

options.text: Oral thiamine 100 mg daily without parenteral loading
options.rationale: Oral thiamine has poor bioavailability in chronic alcohol use due to gut malabsorption; parenteral loading is required for high-risk patients. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Parenteral thiamine 100 mg IV once, then oral 100 mg daily
options.rationale: 100 mg single dose is below the prophylactic threshold for at-risk patients; 300 mg IV daily for 3 days is the standard Australian regimen. Would be inadequate (Source: NSW Health).

options.text: Glucose 5% IV first to restore blood sugar, then thiamine
options.rationale: Glucose given before thiamine in a deficient brain precipitates Wernicke encephalopathy by accelerating thiamine consumption. Would be unsafe (Source: eTG).

options.text: Wait for clinical features of Wernicke (the classic triad) before initiating thiamine
options.rationale: The classic triad (ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, confusion) is present in only ~10% of cases; prophylaxis is required in high-risk patients to prevent irreversible Korsakoff syndrome. Would be unsafe (Source: eTG).

explanation.summary: Wernicke encephalopathy is an acute thiamine-deficiency syndrome (classic triad: ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, confusion - though all three present in only ~10%), at risk of permanent Korsakoff syndrome if untreated. Prophylaxis in high-risk patients (alcohol use disorder, malnutrition, hyperemesis, bariatric surgery) is parenteral thiamine 300 mg IV daily for 3 days, then oral 100 mg daily for 3-6 months. Thiamine MUST precede any glucose load. Anaphylaxis to IV thiamine is rare; oral B-complex is insufficient in alcohol-induced malabsorption.
key_points: 300 mg IV daily x 3 days prophylactic in high-risk
Thiamine BEFORE glucose, always
Classic triad in ~10% only - low threshold for treatment
Korsakoff is the irreversible end-state
Oral 100 mg daily for 3-6 months after parenteral phase
explanation.pearls: Therapeutic doses for established Wernicke encephalopathy are higher: 500 mg IV three times daily for 2-3 days, then taper - much higher than prophylactic doses.

sources.label: Royal College of Physicians Wernicke guideline

subtopic_detail: Parenteral thiamine prophylaxis dose, route and pre-glucose sequencing

stem: A 49-year-old woman has just completed an inpatient alcohol detoxification at a Hunter New England LHD addiction medicine unit. She has been abstinent for 5 days, is now euthymic, has no significant hepatic dysfunction (LFTs normal), no opioid use, and is committed to long-term abstinence. She is asking which medication will best support her staying abstinent. She does not work in a job with random alcohol testing and lives with a supportive partner.

data_table.Examination: Settled, well; no jaundice, no encephalopathy.
data_table.Investigations: LFT normal, U&E normal, ECG sinus rhythm.

lead_in: Which pharmacological choice has the most consistently demonstrated efficacy for relapse prevention?

options.text: Naltrexone 50 mg orally daily
options.rationale: Oral naltrexone (a mu-opioid receptor antagonist) reduces heavy drinking days and risk of relapse to heavy drinking in alcohol use disorder, with Number Needed to Treat ~12 for return-to-any-drinking and ~20 for return-to-heavy-drinking (Jonas et al 2014 JAMA meta-analysis). Mechanism: blocking endogenous opioid reward from alcohol consumption. Contraindicated in concurrent opioid use (precipitated withdrawal) and severe hepatic dysfunction. PBS-listed for AUD in Australia.
source_refs: NHMRC Australian guidelines for the treatment of alcohol problems 2021
Jonas DE et al. Pharmacotherapy for AUD. JAMA 2014

options.text: Acamprosate 666 mg three times daily
options.rationale: Acamprosate also has efficacy (NNT ~12 for abstinence maintenance) and is reasonable; naltrexone has slightly broader evidence for reducing heavy drinking. Acamprosate is preferred if abstinence-not-reduction is the goal and renal function is good. Would be wrong as 'most consistently demonstrated' but a reasonable alternative (Source: Jonas 2014).

options.text: Disulfiram 200 mg daily
options.rationale: Disulfiram works via aversive disulfiram-ethanol reaction (acetaldehyde accumulation) - evidence is mixed and depends on supervised adherence; effective in supervised settings but less robust overall. Would be wrong as 'most consistently demonstrated' (Source: NHMRC 2021).

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg daily
options.rationale: SSRIs do not have demonstrated efficacy for AUD relapse prevention in the absence of comorbid depression. Would be wrong (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Topiramate 100 mg daily
options.rationale: Topiramate has growing evidence but is off-label in Australia for AUD and has cognitive and renal-stone tolerability issues; positioned second-line. Would be wrong as first-line (Source: NHMRC).

explanation.summary: Three pharmacotherapies are PBS-listed in Australia for AUD relapse prevention: naltrexone, acamprosate, and disulfiram. Naltrexone (mu-opioid antagonist) reduces heavy drinking; acamprosate (NMDA modulator/GABAergic) supports abstinence maintenance; disulfiram (aldehyde dehydrogenase inhibitor) creates aversive reaction with alcohol. Naltrexone and acamprosate are evidence-first-line; disulfiram is supervised-adherence-dependent. Combined psychosocial therapy improves outcomes for all three. Second-line: topiramate, gabapentin, baclofen (off-label in Australia).
key_points: First-line: naltrexone (heavy drinking reduction) or acamprosate (abstinence maintenance)
Disulfiram: aversive, requires supervised adherence
All three PBS-listed for AUD in Australia
Naltrexone contraindicated in concurrent opioid use
Combined with psychosocial therapy for best outcomes
explanation.pearls: Naltrexone before drinking ('targeted dosing' on heavy-drinking days) is evidence-supported for harm reduction rather than abstinence; this option fits patients not committed to total abstinence.

sources.label: NHMRC Australian guidelines for alcohol problems 2021

subtopic_detail: Naltrexone first-line for AUD relapse prevention

stem: A 56-year-old man with alcohol use disorder asks his GP at a Central Coast LHD practice about acamprosate, which he has read may help him stay sober. He has Stage 3 chronic kidney disease (eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73 m2), normal LFTs, no opioid use, and weighs 90 kg.

data_table.Examination: Settled.
data_table.Investigations: eGFR 38, LFT normal, electrolytes normal.

lead_in: Which is the most accurate response regarding suitability and choice?

options.text: Acamprosate is contraindicated in significant renal impairment; consider naltrexone instead given normal liver function
options.rationale: Acamprosate is renally excreted unchanged; it is contraindicated when creatinine clearance is below 30 mL/min and cautioned in mild-moderate impairment with dose reduction or avoidance. eGFR 38 is borderline and most prescribers would prefer naltrexone (hepatic metabolism, normal LFTs here) given the renal context. Naltrexone is contraindicated in current opioid use (precipitated withdrawal) and severe hepatic dysfunction, neither of which applies.

options.text: Use full-dose acamprosate; renal excretion is not relevant
options.rationale: Acamprosate is renally cleared and dose-relevant in renal impairment. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Add disulfiram alongside acamprosate routinely
options.rationale: Routine combination is not standard; choose a single agent based on patient profile. Would be wrong (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Naltrexone is contraindicated in renal impairment
options.rationale: Naltrexone is metabolised hepatically; renal impairment does not contraindicate it. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Pharmacotherapy is contraindicated whenever any comorbidity exists
options.rationale: Comorbidity informs choice but does not contraindicate all pharmacotherapy. Would be wrong (Source: NHMRC).

explanation.summary: Acamprosate is renally excreted unchanged; contraindicated when creatinine clearance is below 30 mL/min, dose-reduced or avoided in mild-moderate impairment. Naltrexone is hepatically metabolised; contraindicated in severe hepatic dysfunction or current opioid use. Choice of relapse-prevention pharmacotherapy is shaped by these comorbidities: liver disease without opioids favours acamprosate; renal disease without opioids favours naltrexone.
key_points: Acamprosate renally excreted; CrCl <30 contraindicated
Naltrexone hepatically metabolised; severe liver disease contraindicated
Current opioid use contraindicates naltrexone (precipitated withdrawal)
Comorbidity shapes choice, not blanket contraindication
Disulfiram requires supervision and motivation
explanation.pearls: Acamprosate has the unusual property of being almost completely unmetabolised - it is excreted unchanged in urine; this makes drug-drug interactions minimal but renal function critical.

subtopic_detail: Choice between naltrexone and acamprosate by organ-dysfunction profile

stem: A 35-year-old man with a 10-year history of heroin use disorder presents to a Hunter New England LHD opioid treatment program clinic seeking opioid substitution therapy (OST). He has tried methadone twice in the past with significant sedation, mild QT prolongation noted on prior maintenance, and intermittent diversion concerns. He works in construction and asks about the alternative.

data_table.Examination: Well at present, no current intoxication.
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus rhythm QTc 470 ms; urine drug screen positive opioids.

lead_in: Which OST option is most appropriate given his history?

options.text: Buprenorphine-naloxone (Suboxone) sublingual film
options.rationale: Buprenorphine (partial mu-opioid agonist) has a ceiling effect on respiratory depression making it safer in overdose, less QT impact than methadone, lower sedation, and the combined buprenorphine-naloxone product (Suboxone) discourages diversion via injection (naloxone has poor sublingual bioavailability but precipitates withdrawal if injected). His previous methadone sedation and QT prolongation favour switching. NSW OST clinics can supervise induction.
source_refs: NSW Health Opioid Treatment Program clinical guidelines

options.text: Methadone 30 mg daily
options.rationale: Methadone has caused QT prolongation and sedation in this patient; baseline QTc 470 ms is borderline and another methadone trial risks torsades. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Oral naltrexone for relapse prevention
options.rationale: Naltrexone requires complete opioid abstinence before initiation (would precipitate withdrawal); not appropriate as immediate OST. Would be wrong timing (Source: AMH).

options.text: Slow-release oral morphine
options.rationale: Slow-release morphine is a niche third-line OST option in Australia, used when methadone and buprenorphine have failed; not first switch. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Codeine 60 mg four times daily as a substitution
options.rationale: Codeine is not an evidence-based OST; not part of the Australian OTP. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health).

explanation.summary: Australian Opioid Treatment Program offers methadone (full mu-agonist), buprenorphine or buprenorphine-naloxone (partial mu-agonist), and depot buprenorphine (Sublocade, Buvidal). Methadone causes more sedation and QT prolongation; buprenorphine has a ceiling effect on respiratory depression (safer in overdose), less QT, lower sedation. Buprenorphine-naloxone reduces diversion via injection (naloxone precipitates withdrawal if injected). Induction requires sufficient opioid abstinence (~12-24 h since last short-acting opioid) to avoid precipitated withdrawal.
key_points: Methadone: full agonist, no ceiling on respiratory depression, QT risk
Buprenorphine: partial agonist, ceiling effect on respiratory depression
Buprenorphine-naloxone reduces diversion via injection
Induction needs opioid abstinence (12-24h short-acting, longer for methadone)
Depot buprenorphine (weekly/monthly) improves adherence
explanation.pearls: Buprenorphine's ceiling effect on respiratory depression is the main safety advantage over methadone - it explains the divergence of overdose mortality between the two when comparing OTP cohorts.

subtopic_detail: Buprenorphine-naloxone over methadone given QT and sedation history

stem: A 28-year-old woman is being inducted onto buprenorphine-naloxone at a Hunter New England LHD opioid treatment program clinic. Her last heroin use was 4 hours ago. She is currently exhibiting no opioid withdrawal symptoms.

data_table.Examination: Calm, no diaphoresis, no piloerection, no rhinorrhoea, no yawning, pupil size normal.
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen positive opioids.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate action?

options.text: Delay buprenorphine induction until she shows objective withdrawal features (typically COWS score >=12), expected approximately 12-24 hours after last short-acting opioid
options.rationale: Buprenorphine is a high-affinity partial mu-agonist that displaces full agonists (heroin, methadone) from receptors. If administered while a full agonist still occupies receptors, the substitution reduces net opioid activity, precipitating withdrawal. Standard practice is to wait until objective withdrawal is present (Clinical Opiate Withdrawal Scale - COWS - score >=12) before starting buprenorphine, typically 12-24 hours after last short-acting opioid and longer (36-72 hours or more) after methadone.

options.text: Give buprenorphine 4 mg sublingual now
options.rationale: Administering before withdrawal is established risks precipitated withdrawal, with abrupt distressing symptoms. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Start methadone 30 mg immediately instead
options.rationale: Methadone induction also requires careful titration; switching the agent does not solve the timing issue and was not the agreed plan. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health OTP).

options.text: Administer naloxone first to clear residual opioid
options.rationale: Naloxone would precipitate immediate severe withdrawal and is not part of induction; nonsensical here. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Administer buprenorphine 0.4 mg test dose now
options.rationale: Even small doses of buprenorphine can precipitate withdrawal in a recently-dosed opioid user; the protocol is to wait, not to test with micro-dose in standard induction. Note: micro-dosing protocols exist but are specialist-led and not first-line for typical heroin users (Source: NSW Health OTP).

explanation.summary: Buprenorphine induction timing prevents precipitated withdrawal. Buprenorphine's high mu-receptor affinity but partial agonism means it can displace a full agonist (heroin, methadone) and reduce net opioid activity, abruptly precipitating withdrawal. Protocol: wait until COWS >=12 (~12-24 h after short-acting opioid, 36-72+ h after methadone), then induce with small initial doses (e.g. 2-4 mg) titrated upward. Micro-dosing protocols (Bernese method) are specialist-led alternatives for patients who cannot tolerate withdrawal before induction.
key_points: Buprenorphine partial agonist + high affinity = precipitated withdrawal risk
Wait for COWS >=12 before induction
12-24 h post short-acting opioid; 36-72+ h post methadone
Start 2-4 mg sublingual, titrate by symptoms
Micro-dosing (Bernese) is specialist option
explanation.pearls: Precipitated withdrawal is distressing and can prematurely end an induction attempt - patient engagement and trust are damaged; cautious timing is therapeutic alliance protection.

subtopic_detail: Buprenorphine induction timing - COWS-guided

stem: A 30-year-old man is found unresponsive in a Hunter New England LHD ED ambulance bay. He has pinpoint pupils, RR 6, SpO2 82%. Paramedic handover reports a needle and small bag of brown powder beside him. He weighs approximately 75 kg.

data_table.Examination: GCS 6, pinpoint pupils, shallow respirations, no obvious trauma, no track marks visible above clothing.
data_table.Investigations: Finger-prick glucose 6.0 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Bag-valve-mask ventilation with supplemental oxygen, and IV naloxone 0.1-0.4 mg titrated to adequate ventilation (not full consciousness)
options.rationale: Opioid overdose causes death by respiratory depression. Immediate priority is ventilation; naloxone (mu-opioid receptor antagonist) is titrated in small aliquots (0.1-0.4 mg IV) to restore adequate respiration (RR >=12, SpO2 acceptable), not to full consciousness. Full waking precipitates abrupt withdrawal, distress, and patient self-discharge before safe redosing as naloxone half-life (60-90 min) is shorter than most opioids. Repeat boluses or infusion may be needed.
source_refs: NSW Poisons Information Centre Opioid overdose

options.text: IV naloxone 2 mg bolus immediately
options.rationale: Large bolus precipitates abrupt withdrawal, vomiting (aspiration), agitation, and patient self-discharge before redose. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Intubate and ventilate immediately without naloxone
options.rationale: Naloxone is the targeted reversal agent and avoids intubation when effective; ventilation is the bridge, not the destination. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: IV flumazenil to reverse the overdose
options.rationale: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines, not opioids; pinpoint pupils + bradypnoea = opioid toxidrome. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Activated charcoal via nasogastric tube
options.rationale: Activated charcoal is for recent oral overdose with intact airway; this is parenteral overdose with airway compromise. Would be wrong (Source: NSW PIC).

explanation.summary: Opioid overdose causes death by respiratory depression; naloxone (mu-opioid antagonist) is the targeted reversal. Titrate in small aliquots (0.1-0.4 mg IV) to restore adequate ventilation (RR >=12), not to full consciousness, to avoid precipitated withdrawal and patient self-discharge. Naloxone half-life (60-90 min) is shorter than most opioids (especially long-acting agents like methadone), so observation, repeat boluses, or infusion may be needed. Take-home naloxone (intranasal Nyxoid, Prenoxad IM) is a key harm reduction intervention.
key_points: Opioid toxidrome: pinpoint pupils + bradypnoea + reduced consciousness
Titrate naloxone 0.1-0.4 mg IV; goal is ventilation, not waking
Naloxone half-life shorter than most opioids - observation required
Methadone and long-acting opioids: prolonged monitoring
Take-home naloxone is high-yield harm reduction
explanation.pearls: Methadone overdose can re-sedate after initial naloxone wears off; ICU observation up to 24 hours may be required, sometimes with naloxone infusion.

subtopic_detail: Naloxone titration in opioid overdose

stem: A 27-year-old man is brought into a Hunter New England LHD ED by police at 0200 with acute paranoid agitation. He has been using crystal methamphetamine for 48 hours. He is currently shouting at the corner of the cubicle that 'they are in the walls', is sweating and tachycardic, and has clenched fists. He has no prior psychiatric history. Verbal de-escalation has failed; he has accepted no oral medication. He weighs 80 kg.

data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, mydriasis, hyperalert; HR 128, BP 168/96, RR 22, SpO2 99%, temp 38.0.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 6.0 mmol/L; ECG sinus tachycardia, QTc 420 ms; urine drug screen pending.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial pharmacological intervention?

options.text: IM olanzapine 10 mg with IV access being established for benzodiazepine titration
options.rationale: In methamphetamine-induced acute behavioural disturbance with combined psychosis and sympathomimetic features, NSW Health and HNE LHD pathways support either IM olanzapine (preferred in this scenario where psychotic features are prominent and IV access is not yet available) or IV benzodiazepine titration once access is in place. The two are not combined within 1 hour due to combined cardiorespiratory depression risk. The plan is to secure IV access in parallel and add titrated benzodiazepine after the olanzapine window if sympathomimetic features predominate.
source_refs: NSW Health PD2015_007 Management of Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in EDs
HNE LHD acute behavioural disturbance pathway

options.text: IM olanzapine 10 mg combined with IM midazolam 5 mg
options.rationale: Combining IM olanzapine with parenteral benzodiazepine within 1 hour increases cardiorespiratory depression and is explicitly avoided in NSW guidance. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health).

options.text: IM haloperidol 10 mg without ECG check
options.rationale: Haloperidol carries higher QT prolongation risk than olanzapine and methamphetamine itself can prolong QT; without ECG support, haloperidol is not preferred. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Active cooling and observation alone
options.rationale: Active cooling is necessary alongside but does not address agitation, sympathetic drive or psychosis; required as adjunct, not alone. Would be incomplete (Source: NSW PIC).

options.text: IV beta-blocker (metoprolol 5 mg) for the hypertension
options.rationale: Beta-blockade in unopposed alpha stimulation (methamphetamine) risks worsening hypertension; avoided. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

explanation.summary: Acute methamphetamine intoxication presents with sympathomimetic features (hyperthermia, hypertension, tachycardia, mydriasis, diaphoresis) plus psychomotor agitation and frequently psychosis. Management: ventilation/airway, active cooling, IV fluids, IV benzodiazepines titrated for the sympathetic drive and seizure prophylaxis, IM olanzapine for the psychotic/agitated component if IV access is unavailable. Do NOT combine IM olanzapine with parenteral benzodiazepine within 1 hour. Avoid beta-blocker monotherapy (unopposed alpha stimulation). Methamphetamine-induced psychosis often resolves over hours to days; persistent psychosis warrants psychiatric review.
key_points: Methamphetamine = sympathomimetic + psychomotor + psychosis
First-line: benzodiazepines IV titrated (when access available)
IM olanzapine for psychotic/agitated component without IV access
Do not combine IM olanzapine with parenteral benzodiazepine within 1 hour
Avoid beta-blocker monotherapy
explanation.pearls: HNE LHD has high rates of methamphetamine presentations; ED training pathways embed these specific drug-on-drug timing rules.

subtopic_detail: Methamphetamine acute psychosis with agitation - sedation pathway

stem: A 24-year-old man presents to a Central Coast LHD ED with acute psychotic features (paranoid delusions, auditory hallucinations) and a positive amphetamine urine drug screen. The on-call team is considering whether to start a long-term antipsychotic and refer to early psychosis services.

data_table.Examination: Persecutory delusions, auditory hallucinations, thought disorder mild.
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen positive amphetamines; no other acute medical issue.

lead_in: Which approach most accurately reflects best practice in distinguishing primary from substance-induced psychosis?

options.text: Treat acute symptoms and observe; reassess for persistence beyond expected stimulant effect period (typically days to a few weeks) before committing to a primary psychosis diagnosis and long-term antipsychotic
options.rationale: Methamphetamine-induced psychosis typically resolves within days to a few weeks of abstinence, with the majority resolving in under a month. DSM-5 distinguishes substance-induced psychotic disorder (resolves within a month of withdrawal) from primary psychotic disorder. Premature commitment to a primary psychosis diagnosis and indefinite antipsychotic may pathologise a self-limiting drug effect; persistence beyond 4 weeks of abstinence (or earlier in the presence of strong primary-illness features) warrants formal primary diagnosis.
source_refs: Bramness JG et al. Amphetamine-induced psychosis review

options.text: Immediately diagnose schizophrenia and start lifelong antipsychotic
options.rationale: Substance-induced psychosis often resolves; premature diagnosis pathologises self-limiting features. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Discharge after sedation with no follow-up since substance is the cause
options.rationale: Follow-up is essential to distinguish substance-induced from primary illness and to engage with substance treatment. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Refuse antipsychotic in the acute phase
options.rationale: Acute antipsychotic for distressing psychotic symptoms during the acute phase is appropriate; the question is about long-term diagnosis and treatment, not acute refusal. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Assume primary psychosis until substance use is proven by repeat testing
options.rationale: Strong positive urine drug screen + temporal relationship is supportive of substance-induced; default-to-primary inverts the diagnostic logic. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

explanation.summary: Substance-induced psychotic disorder (DSM-5) resolves within 1 month of cessation; persistence beyond that, or features of primary psychosis (negative symptoms, prodrome, family history, prior episodes off substance), shift the diagnostic frame to primary psychotic disorder. Best practice is to treat acute symptoms, observe over the substance-withdrawal window, and reassess before committing to a primary diagnosis and long-term antipsychotic. Approximately 20-30% of patients with stimulant-induced psychosis develop a primary psychotic illness on longitudinal follow-up.
key_points: Substance-induced psychosis resolves within 1 month of cessation
Persistence beyond 1 month -> primary diagnosis
20-30% of stimulant-induced psychosis develops primary illness
Acute antipsychotic for distressing symptoms is appropriate
Follow-up engagement essential
explanation.pearls: Cannabis-induced psychosis has a similar pattern but a higher conversion rate to primary psychosis (~40% in some cohorts), particularly in youth onset and high-potency use.

sources.label: Bramness JG et al. Amphetamine-induced psychosis. BMC Psychiatry 2012

subtopic_detail: Substance-induced vs primary psychosis distinction

stem: A GP at a Central Coast LHD primary care practice wants to introduce a routine brief alcohol screening tool at general consultations. She wants the shortest validated instrument with a documented cut-off for hazardous use.

data_table.Examination: N/A teaching scenario.
data_table.Investigations: N/A.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate tool?

options.text: AUDIT-C (Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test - Consumption), 3 items; positive at >=4 for men or >=3 for women
options.rationale: AUDIT-C is the 3-item brief alcohol screen derived from the full 10-item AUDIT, validated in primary care for hazardous drinking detection. Cut-offs for hazardous use are >=4 for men and >=3 for women in most validation studies; higher cut-offs (>=5/>=4) increase specificity for AUD. RACGP and NHMRC guidance support AUDIT-C as a brief opportunistic primary care screen.
source_refs: NHMRC Australian guidelines for alcohol problems 2021
Bush K et al. AUDIT-C validation Arch Intern Med 1998

options.text: CAGE questionnaire, 4 items
options.rationale: CAGE detects alcohol dependence rather than hazardous drinking and is less sensitive in women and young people; AUDIT-C is preferred for screening. Would be less appropriate (Source: RACGP).

options.text: Full AUDIT (10 items) at every consultation
options.rationale: Full AUDIT is longer than needed for a brief screen; AUDIT-C is the brief version specifically designed for primary care. Would be impractical (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: GAD-7
options.rationale: GAD-7 is for anxiety, not alcohol. Would be wrong (Source: Spitzer).

options.text: PHQ-9
options.rationale: PHQ-9 is for depression, not alcohol. Would be wrong (Source: Kroenke).

explanation.summary: AUDIT-C is a 3-item brief screen for hazardous alcohol use, derived from the full 10-item AUDIT. Cut-offs: >=4 for men and >=3 for women for hazardous use; positive screen prompts full AUDIT and brief intervention. RACGP and NHMRC support its use in opportunistic primary care screening. CAGE was an earlier tool focused on dependence rather than hazardous use, with reduced sensitivity in women and young people.
key_points: AUDIT-C: 3 items, brief screen for hazardous use
Cut-off: >=4 men, >=3 women
Positive screen prompts full AUDIT + brief intervention
CAGE outdated; less sensitive in women and youth
Australian standard drink = 10g pure alcohol
explanation.pearls: NHMRC 2021 alcohol guidelines: no more than 10 standard drinks per week and no more than 4 in any single day, for both men and women; this is a shift from the previous sex-differentiated 2009 thresholds.

subtopic_detail: AUDIT-C as brief primary care alcohol screen

stem: A 27-year-old pregnant woman (12 weeks gestation) with a history of intravenous heroin use presents to a Hunter New England LHD antenatal clinic. She is not currently on opioid substitution therapy and is requesting initiation. She has stopped recreational use 3 days ago but is craving.

data_table.Examination: Mild withdrawal features; gravid uterus consistent with dates.
data_table.Investigations: Routine antenatal bloods unremarkable; viral screen negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate OST plan for her pregnancy?

options.text: Initiate methadone or buprenorphine OST in pregnancy under specialist supervision; both are recommended over no OST in opioid-dependent pregnant women
options.rationale: Both methadone and buprenorphine are recommended in pregnancy under specialist supervision; opioid substitution therapy reduces obstetric and neonatal complications compared with continued unstructured use or unsupervised withdrawal. Buprenorphine is associated with somewhat less severe neonatal abstinence syndrome (MOTHER trial 2010, Jones et al NEJM), but methadone has the longer historical evidence base. Choice depends on prior experience, retention, and clinical context. Avoid abrupt unsupervised withdrawal (risk of foetal stress and miscarriage).
source_refs: NSW Health OTP guidelines pregnancy section
Jones HE et al. MOTHER trial NEJM 2010

options.text: Avoid all OST in pregnancy due to foetal risk
options.rationale: OST is recommended in pregnancy; unsupervised withdrawal carries foetal stress and miscarriage risk and ongoing unstructured use carries higher foetal harm. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCOG).

options.text: Use naltrexone in pregnancy to block relapse
options.rationale: Naltrexone is not recommended in pregnancy; lacks safety evidence and risks precipitated withdrawal. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Recommend rapid detoxification under sedation
options.rationale: Rapid detoxification is not recommended in pregnancy; risk of foetal stress and miscarriage. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCOG).

options.text: Defer all intervention until postnatal period
options.rationale: Deferring leaves the mother and foetus exposed to unstructured use; antenatal OST initiation is recommended. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health OTP).

explanation.summary: OST in pregnancy is recommended over no OST in opioid-dependent women; abrupt unsupervised withdrawal risks foetal stress and miscarriage. Both methadone and buprenorphine are acceptable under specialist supervision. The MOTHER trial (Jones HE et al NEJM 2010) showed buprenorphine associated with shorter neonatal abstinence syndrome treatment duration and length of stay compared with methadone, though similar overall obstetric outcomes. Neonatal team awareness for NAS is essential; breastfeeding is generally encouraged while on either agent.
key_points: OST recommended over no OST in opioid-dependent pregnant women
Both methadone and buprenorphine acceptable
Buprenorphine: shorter NAS treatment duration (MOTHER trial)
Avoid rapid detoxification (foetal stress)
Breastfeeding generally encouraged on OST
explanation.pearls: Neonatal abstinence syndrome (NAS) is more about postnatal management (Finnegan scoring, morphine or methadone taper) than antenatal choice; both agents allow successful neonatal weaning.

sources.label: Jones HE et al. MOTHER trial NEJM 2010

subtopic_detail: OST initiation in pregnancy - methadone or buprenorphine

stem: A 26-year-old man presents to a Hunter New England LHD ED in withdrawal after stopping daily GHB use. He had been taking GHB every 2 hours including overnight. He is now diaphoretic, tremulous, tachycardic, and intermittently confused 16 hours after his last dose. He has had two episodes of brief loss of consciousness in the past hour.

data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, tremulous, intermittent confusion, HR 130, BP 168/98, RR 22, SpO2 98%, temp 38.2.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 5.8 mmol/L, electrolytes normal, CK 600 U/L.

lead_in: Which best describes the management approach for GHB withdrawal in this severity?

options.text: Admit to a high-dependency unit, give high-dose benzodiazepines titrated to symptoms, consider barbiturate or propofol if benzodiazepine-refractory, with intensive monitoring
options.rationale: GHB has a short half-life (~30 min) and induces severe withdrawal that can progress rapidly to a delirium with autonomic instability, requiring HDU/ICU-level care. Management is high-dose benzodiazepines titrated to symptoms; benzodiazepine-refractory cases may need barbiturate (phenobarbital) or propofol sedation under intensive care. Mortality without aggressive management is non-trivial. Outpatient withdrawal is not appropriate.
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Guidelines 2008

options.text: Outpatient management with oral diazepam taper
options.rationale: Severe GHB withdrawal is not safely managed as outpatient; admission required. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Naltrexone for relapse prevention started today
options.rationale: Naltrexone is opioid-targeted, not relevant in GHB withdrawal; the priority is acute withdrawal management. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Beta-blocker for the tachycardia and hypertension
options.rationale: Beta-blocker addresses symptoms without treating underlying GABAergic deficit; benzodiazepines are the substrate substitution. Would be wrong as primary therapy (Source: eTG).

options.text: Restart GHB at maintenance dose to avoid withdrawal
options.rationale: GHB has no medical prescribing role for substitution; substitution is via benzodiazepines (GABA-A activity overlap). Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

explanation.summary: GHB (gamma-hydroxybutyrate) is a GABA-B (and weak GABA-A) agonist with a very short half-life; daily-frequent use builds rapid tolerance, and abrupt cessation produces a severe withdrawal syndrome (autonomic instability, agitation, delirium, sometimes psychosis or seizures) that can progress rapidly. Management is inpatient with high-dose benzodiazepine substitution titrated to symptoms; refractory cases may require barbiturate or propofol sedation under HDU/ICU care. The short half-life of GHB means symptoms can escalate within hours of cessation, unlike the longer alcohol withdrawal trajectory.
key_points: GHB has very short half-life - withdrawal escalates fast
Severe withdrawal can be delirium with autonomic storm
Management: inpatient + high-dose benzodiazepines + HDU/ICU monitoring
Refractory: phenobarbital or propofol
Outpatient withdrawal unsafe
explanation.pearls: GHB withdrawal is often under-recognised in EDs because the patient may not disclose use; clinicians should consider GHB withdrawal when a young patient presents with severe agitation/autonomic features and no obvious alcohol or stimulant history.

subtopic_detail: Severe GHB withdrawal - inpatient HDU benzodiazepine titration

stem: A 60-year-old woman has been on diazepam 10 mg three times daily for 15 years for 'nerves'. Her GP at a Central Coast LHD practice plans to taper her off due to falls risk and a recent hip fracture. She agrees to taper.

data_table.Examination: Mild gait instability; otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: Bone density T-score -2.8 at femoral neck.

lead_in: Which tapering approach most closely follows Australian guidance?

options.text: Slow gradual reduction of approximately 10% of the current dose every 2-4 weeks, with longer plateaus at lower doses, allowing 6-12 months or more for completion
options.rationale: Long-term benzodiazepine withdrawal requires very gradual tapering to minimise seizures, rebound anxiety/insomnia, and protracted withdrawal symptoms. Australian and Ashton-manual-based guidance suggests reducing by approximately 10% of the current dose every 2-4 weeks, with longer plateaus at lower doses (because each mg drop is a larger proportion). Total taper duration is typically 6-12+ months for long-term users. Switching to a long-acting equivalent (diazepam) before tapering is helpful for short-acting agents (lorazepam, oxazepam, alprazolam).
source_refs: Ashton C. Benzodiazepines: how they work and how to withdraw

options.text: Stop diazepam immediately and use lorazepam PRN for breakthrough symptoms
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation after 15 years risks seizures and severe rebound; gradual taper required. Would be wrong (Source: RACGP).

options.text: Halve the dose weekly until off
options.rationale: 50% weekly reduction is too rapid for long-term users; symptoms and seizure risk are substantial. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Add propranolol and stop the diazepam in 1 week
options.rationale: Propranolol masks autonomic symptoms but does not prevent seizures; rapid cessation is still unsafe. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Switch to alprazolam first as it is easier to taper
options.rationale: Alprazolam is short-acting and more difficult to taper; switching FROM diazepam TO alprazolam inverts the recommended approach. Would be wrong (Source: Ashton).

explanation.summary: Long-term benzodiazepine cessation requires gradual taper (~10% of current dose every 2-4 weeks, longer plateaus at lower doses, total 6-12+ months) to minimise seizure risk and rebound symptoms. Switching short-acting agents (alprazolam, lorazepam, oxazepam) to diazepam-equivalent first smooths the taper because of diazepam's long half-life. Psychological support reduces relapse. Patients often experience protracted withdrawal symptoms (mood, sleep, cognitive) for weeks to months beyond the taper.
key_points: ~10% reduction of current dose every 2-4 weeks
Longer plateaus at lower doses (proportional reductions get larger)
Total taper 6-12+ months for long-term users
Switch short-acting to diazepam first if needed
Psychological support reduces relapse
explanation.pearls: The Ashton manual is the most widely referenced patient-facing tapering resource; many GPs share it with patients during deprescribing.

subtopic_detail: Gradual benzodiazepine deprescribing in long-term user

stem: A 19-year-old man attends a Hunter New England LHD youth health clinic seeking help to stop cannabis. He has been smoking 1-2 g daily for 4 years and tried to stop a month ago but became irritable, anxious, slept poorly with vivid dreams, lost his appetite, and resumed within 4 days. He has no other substance use.

data_table.Examination: Unremarkable; weight steady.
data_table.Investigations: Nil acute.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management approach?

options.text: Evidence-based psychological intervention (CBT and/or motivational interviewing) plus symptomatic management of cannabis withdrawal symptoms
options.rationale: Cannabis withdrawal (irritability, anxiety, sleep disturbance with vivid dreams, appetite loss, restlessness) typically peaks at days 2-4 and resolves within 1-2 weeks; recognition prevents relapse. First-line management is psychological (CBT, motivational enhancement, contingency management) with symptomatic support; no pharmacotherapy is approved as first-line in Australia for cannabis use disorder. Nabiximols and other agents have been trialled but are not standard practice.
source_refs: NHMRC Australian guidelines for cannabis treatment
Allsop DJ et al. Cannabis Withdrawal Scale

options.text: Start naltrexone for relapse prevention
options.rationale: Naltrexone is for alcohol and opioid use disorders, not cannabis. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Replace cannabis with prescribed medicinal cannabis indefinitely
options.rationale: Medicinal cannabis as substitution therapy for cannabis use disorder is not standard practice and lacks evidence-based positioning. Would be wrong (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Long-term diazepam for withdrawal symptoms
options.rationale: Long-term benzodiazepines create dependence; not first-line for cannabis withdrawal. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Quetiapine 50 mg nocte for sleep
options.rationale: Quetiapine off-label for sleep is not appropriate; brief sleep hygiene and short-term benzodiazepine alternative (e.g. mirtazapine if needed) are preferred. Would be wrong as first-line (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Cannabis use disorder is treated first-line with psychological intervention (CBT, motivational enhancement, contingency management). Cannabis withdrawal (irritability, anxiety, sleep disturbance, vivid dreams, appetite change, restlessness, craving) is recognised in DSM-5; peaks days 2-4, resolves 1-2 weeks. No pharmacotherapy is first-line in Australia; symptomatic support and engagement with psychological treatment are core. Acknowledgement that withdrawal is real and time-limited reduces relapse.
key_points: First-line: CBT, motivational enhancement, contingency management
Cannabis withdrawal is DSM-5 recognised - real, time-limited
Peaks days 2-4, resolves 1-2 weeks
No first-line pharmacotherapy in Australia
Validation of withdrawal symptoms reduces relapse
explanation.pearls: Vivid dreams in cannabis withdrawal reflect REM-rebound after chronic suppression of REM sleep; warning patients about this in advance frames the symptom as expected, not catastrophic.

subtopic_detail: Cannabis use disorder first-line management

stem: A 38-year-old Aboriginal man attends a Hunter New England LHD Aboriginal Medical Service clinic with alcohol use disorder. He drinks ~150 g pure alcohol most days, has previously had brief abstinence with relapse, and is now ready to engage in treatment. He has no significant hepatic dysfunction, no concurrent opioid use, and is supported by his family.

data_table.Examination: Settled, hepatomegaly absent; mood low-stable.
data_table.Investigations: LFT normal, FBC normal, GGT 90 U/L.

lead_in: Which combination most aligns with culturally safe best-practice care?

options.text: Pharmacotherapy choice (acamprosate or naltrexone) plus culturally safe psychosocial care via the Aboriginal Medical Service and involvement of family with consent
options.rationale: NHMRC 2021 alcohol guidelines and Aboriginal-specific resources support evidence-based pharmacotherapy (naltrexone or acamprosate) alongside culturally safe psychosocial care delivered through Aboriginal Community Controlled Health Services where possible. Engagement of family with consent reinforces support networks central to Aboriginal cultural frameworks. AMS settings reduce barriers and improve retention.
source_refs: NHMRC Australian guidelines for alcohol problems 2021
RACGP National guide to a preventive health assessment for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people

options.text: Restrict treatment to abstinence-only without pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is evidence-based for AUD and access should not be restricted by demographic. Would be wrong (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Mandatory inpatient detoxification in a tertiary hospital remote from his community
options.rationale: Mandatory removal from community is not culturally safe; community-based care where possible improves engagement. Would be wrong (Source: NACCHO).

options.text: Disulfiram as first-line without prior abstinence
options.rationale: Disulfiram requires established abstinence before initiation and supervised adherence; not first-line as initial approach. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Brief intervention only without pharmacotherapy or longitudinal care
options.rationale: Brief intervention is appropriate for hazardous use but inadequate for established AUD with previous relapse; longitudinal pharmacological + psychosocial care is required. Would be wrong (Source: NHMRC).

explanation.summary: Best-practice AUD care for Aboriginal patients integrates evidence-based pharmacotherapy with culturally safe psychosocial care, ideally delivered through Aboriginal Community Controlled Health Services (Aboriginal Medical Services). Community-based care, family involvement with consent, and recognition of intergenerational trauma improve engagement. Pharmacotherapy access should not be restricted by demographic. NHMRC and NACCHO guidance align on these principles.
key_points: Evidence-based pharmacotherapy + culturally safe psychosocial care
AMS settings improve engagement and retention
Family involvement with consent
Recognise intergenerational trauma
Avoid removal from community where possible
explanation.pearls: Aboriginal Community Controlled Health Services have demonstrated better engagement and outcomes than mainstream services for many conditions; refer locally where available.

subtopic_detail: Culturally safe AUD care via Aboriginal Medical Service

stem: A 52-year-old man on disulfiram 200 mg daily for alcohol use disorder maintenance is admitted to a Central Coast LHD ward for community-acquired pneumonia. The admitting team is reviewing his medication chart and notes a planned course of metronidazole 400 mg orally three times daily for an unrelated dental abscess. He is not currently consuming alcohol.

data_table.Examination: Mild respiratory crackles in right lower lobe; otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: Bloods unremarkable; chest X-ray confirms RLL consolidation.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate action regarding the planned metronidazole?

options.text: Avoid metronidazole in combination with disulfiram due to risk of acute confusional/psychotic state; choose an alternative antimicrobial
options.rationale: Metronidazole combined with disulfiram has been associated with acute confusion, psychosis and seizures (the mechanism is thought to involve compound dopamine-beta-hydroxylase inhibition leading to dopamine accumulation and noradrenaline depletion in the CNS). The combination is contraindicated in many references; alternative antimicrobials should be chosen. This is independent of the well-known disulfiram-ethanol reaction (acetaldehyde accumulation when alcohol is consumed on disulfiram).

options.text: Use both medications as planned
options.rationale: Combination has caused confusional psychosis and is to be avoided; not safe. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Stop disulfiram for the duration of the metronidazole course only
options.rationale: Disulfiram has prolonged effect (up to 14 days post-cessation); brief pause does not eliminate interaction risk and disrupts AUD maintenance. Choosing an alternative antimicrobial is preferred. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Stop disulfiram permanently regardless
options.rationale: Permanent cessation removes a maintenance therapy without need; switching the antibiotic is the simpler, safer change. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Add a benzodiazepine prophylactically to cover the interaction
options.rationale: Benzodiazepine prophylaxis does not prevent the disulfiram-metronidazole CNS effect and adds its own risks. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Disulfiram has multiple important interactions beyond the well-known disulfiram-ethanol reaction (acetaldehyde accumulation when alcohol is consumed). Metronidazole + disulfiram has been associated with acute confusional/psychotic states; the combination is to be avoided. Other relevant interactions include warfarin (increased INR), phenytoin (raised levels), and isoniazid (CNS toxicity). Disulfiram's effect persists up to 14 days after cessation due to irreversible aldehyde dehydrogenase inhibition.
key_points: Disulfiram + metronidazole: confusional/psychotic state - avoid
Disulfiram effect persists up to 14 days after cessation
Other key interactions: warfarin, phenytoin, isoniazid
Disulfiram-ethanol reaction is the named one but not the only one
Choose alternative antibiotic; do not pause disulfiram briefly
explanation.pearls: Hidden alcohol in pharmaceuticals (some elixirs, mouthwashes, cough syrups) can precipitate disulfiram-ethanol reaction; patient education and pharmacy alert are part of care.

subtopic_detail: Disulfiram-metronidazole interaction beyond the alcohol reaction

stem: A 48-year-old man attends his GP at a Central Coast LHD practice. He smokes 25 cigarettes daily, has tried twice to quit with patches alone, and is committed to a fresh quit attempt. He has no history of psychiatric illness, no cardiovascular events, and asks about the most effective pharmacotherapy.

data_table.Examination: Unremarkable; BP 124/78.
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus rhythm.

lead_in: Which is the most effective pharmacotherapy option?

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg daily, titrated per standard schedule, with quit date in week 2
options.rationale: Varenicline (alpha-4-beta-2 nicotinic partial agonist) is the most effective single pharmacotherapy for smoking cessation (~3x odds of quitting vs placebo), outperforming single-form NRT and bupropion in head-to-head Cochrane network meta-analyses (Cahill et al). Earlier safety signals for neuropsychiatric events were not confirmed in the EAGLES trial (Anthenelli et al NEJM 2016). Combined NRT (patch + faster-onset gum/lozenge) approaches varenicline efficacy and is the second choice.
source_refs: Cahill K et al. Cochrane network meta-analysis 2013
Anthenelli RM et al. EAGLES trial NEJM 2016

options.text: Patch alone, same approach as previous failed attempts
options.rationale: Single-form NRT alone has been tried twice without success; escalation to combined NRT or varenicline is appropriate. Would be wrong (Source: RACGP).

options.text: Bupropion 150 mg twice daily as monotherapy
options.rationale: Bupropion is effective but less so than varenicline or combined NRT; appropriate as alternative. Would be less optimal first choice (Source: Cahill 2013).

options.text: E-cigarettes as replacement indefinitely
options.rationale: E-cigarettes are not first-line pharmacotherapy in Australia; varenicline and NRT are evidence-based first-line. Would be wrong (Source: RACGP).

options.text: Hypnosis as the primary modality
options.rationale: Hypnosis lacks robust evidence as the primary modality; pharmacotherapy is recommended. Would be wrong (Source: Cochrane).

explanation.summary: Varenicline (alpha-4-beta-2 nicotinic partial agonist) has the strongest single-pharmacotherapy evidence for smoking cessation (~3x quitting odds vs placebo). Combined NRT (patch + faster-onset form) approaches varenicline efficacy. Earlier neuropsychiatric safety concerns (which led to a US boxed warning) were not confirmed in the EAGLES trial (Anthenelli NEJM 2016) and the warning was removed. Australian RACGP guidelines now position varenicline and combined NRT as first-line.
key_points: Varenicline most effective single agent (~3x placebo)
Combined NRT (patch + faster-onset) close second
Bupropion alternative
EAGLES (2016) cleared neuropsychiatric concerns
Behavioural support adds to all pharmacotherapy
explanation.pearls: Set the quit date for day 8-14 of varenicline therapy; patient smokes the first week to build receptor occupancy before quit day - this is the licensed protocol.

sources.label: Anthenelli RM et al. EAGLES. NEJM 2016

subtopic_detail: Varenicline vs combined NRT vs bupropion for cessation

stem: A 32-year-old man on methadone 60 mg daily for the past 3 years attends his Hunter New England LHD OST clinic and asks to withdraw from opioid treatment. He has been stable for 2 years and is supported by his partner. He is willing to engage in slow taper.

data_table.Examination: Settled; no current withdrawal.
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen positive methadone only; no other illicits.

lead_in: Which approach to withdrawal is most likely to succeed?

options.text: Very gradual methadone taper (e.g. 2-5 mg every 2-4 weeks, slower at lower doses) with ongoing psychosocial support and consideration of post-taper naltrexone
options.rationale: Successful opioid maintenance cessation requires very gradual tapering (typically 2-5 mg every 2-4 weeks, with longer plateaus and smaller reductions at lower doses) and continued psychosocial engagement. Relapse risk is high (>=60-80% in many cohorts) regardless of pace, but slower tapers and continued support reduce attrition. Naltrexone may be considered after complete cessation to reduce relapse, but requires 7-10 days opioid-free first to avoid precipitated withdrawal.

options.text: Rapid taper over 2 weeks under outpatient supervision
options.rationale: Rapid taper has high relapse rate; not recommended for long-term users. Would be wrong (Source: NSW OTP).

options.text: Switch to buprenorphine first then immediate cessation
options.rationale: Switching to buprenorphine before cessation can be appropriate to ease taper but immediate cessation post-switch is not the recommended approach; gradual taper still needed. Would be wrong as plan (Source: eTG).

options.text: Ultra-rapid opioid detoxification under general anaesthetic
options.rationale: Ultra-rapid detoxification under anaesthesia is no longer recommended (high relapse, complications, deaths reported). Would be wrong (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Stop cold turkey with naltrexone bridge
options.rationale: Naltrexone after cold turkey requires 7-10 opioid-free days; starting on top of methadone precipitates severe withdrawal. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Successful cessation of methadone maintenance requires very gradual taper (2-5 mg every 2-4 weeks, smaller decrements at lower doses) and continued psychosocial engagement. Relapse rates are high regardless of pace (60-80% in most cohorts) - patients should be counselled about this and offered re-engagement with OST without judgment if they relapse. Naltrexone post-cessation can reduce relapse but requires 7-10 days opioid-free first.
key_points: Very gradual taper: 2-5 mg every 2-4 weeks
Slower at lower doses; longer plateaus
Relapse high (60-80%) regardless of pace
Naltrexone option post-cessation (7-10 days opioid-free first)
Ultra-rapid detoxification under anaesthesia not recommended
explanation.pearls: Patient-driven taper with the ability to pause or reverse the decrement when struggling has the best retention; rigid externally-imposed tapers fail more often.

subtopic_detail: Slow methadone taper for opioid cessation

stem: A patient at a Hunter New England LHD primary care clinic asks how many standard drinks are in a 750 mL bottle of wine at 13% alcohol by volume.

data_table.Examination: N/A.
data_table.Investigations: N/A.

lead_in: Approximately how many Australian standard drinks does this contain?

options.text: Approximately 8 standard drinks
options.rationale: An Australian standard drink contains 10 g of pure alcohol. Calculation: 750 mL x 0.13 x 0.789 g/mL (ethanol density) = approximately 77 g pure alcohol, which equates to approximately 7.7 standard drinks (rounded to 8). This is a foundational calculation for alcohol counselling, and underpins NHMRC 2021 guideline limits (<= 10 standard drinks/week and <= 4 on any single day for both men and women).

options.text: Approximately 2 standard drinks
options.rationale: 2 standard drinks would be ~20 g alcohol, which is far below the actual alcohol content of a 750 mL bottle at 13%. Would be wrong on arithmetic (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Approximately 4 standard drinks
options.rationale: Underestimate; 40 g alcohol corresponds to roughly half a bottle at 13%. Would be wrong (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Approximately 12 standard drinks
options.rationale: Overestimate; 12 standard drinks = 120 g alcohol, which is more than a 750 mL bottle at 13%. Would be wrong (Source: NHMRC).

options.text: Approximately 15 standard drinks
options.rationale: Substantial overestimate; not plausible for 750 mL at 13%. Would be wrong (Source: NHMRC).

explanation.summary: An Australian standard drink contains 10 g of pure alcohol. To estimate the standard drinks in any beverage: volume (mL) x %ABV x 0.789 (ethanol density g/mL) divided by 10. For a 750 mL bottle at 13% ABV: 750 x 0.13 x 0.789 / 10 = 7.7 standard drinks (~8). NHMRC 2021 guidelines limit healthy adults to no more than 10 standard drinks per week and no more than 4 in any single day.
key_points: 1 standard drink Australia = 10 g pure alcohol
Calculation: mL x %ABV x 0.789 / 10
Wine 750 mL bottle at 13% ~ 8 standard drinks
NHMRC 2021: <=10/week, <=4/day for both sexes
Always include the calculation in alcohol counselling
explanation.pearls: Patients consistently underestimate their consumption when using 'glasses' as the unit - explicit calculation of standard drinks per typical pour reveals the actual intake.

subtopic_detail: Standard drink calculation for typical wine bottle
